# Transcrição: Lesson 03 - The Habits Loop and How Habits Work

- **Fonte:** Lesson 03 - The Habits Loop and How Habits Work
- **Duração:** 02:05
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:05]

In order to understand how habits work, we need to talk about a common model that psychologists have been using for decades. And that's a three-step model of understanding how habits are formed and the different cycle that they go through. This is often called the habit loop and it has three different steps. The first step is the trigger or the cue, the thing that prompts the behavior. There's some cause that leads to a habit. So step one is the trigger. Step two in the cycle is the routine, the actual habit itself, the behavior that you perform. And then step three is the reward. There's some kind of benefit. There's some effect, some outcome that happens after this habit has occurred. And so this three-step model of trigger, routine, reward is something that we need to keep in mind as we go throughout the rest of the course. Pretty much all habits follow this three-step model and you'll see it come up time and time again. You'll see different pieces of it come up again. We'll talk a lot about triggers, for example, in a variety of different lessons. And so as you continue to think about the habits that you want to build, think about the things that come before them, the triggers that spark them. Think about the actual habit itself, what that routine looks like, how we can improve it, how we can adjust it, which we'll talk about more in the next lesson. And then also about the reward and the benefit that comes from that behavior, the thing that you get from it. So to give you an example, if your phone rings, that's a trigger, right? It's the cue that prompts you to tell you to do the habit of picking up your phone and answering it. So the phone rings, that's a trigger, you answer the phone, that's the routine. And then the reward is you get to satisfy your curiosity for why the person is calling or get new information, you understand what's happening. And so this benefit that you get, that you collect new information, that you get some kind of connection with the person who called you, maybe it's a friend or a family member. And what that tells your brain is, hey, remember when the phone rings, next time we're going to answer it because we've got a positive benefit from this behavior last time. And if that cycle repeats itself enough, then it kind of becomes mindless and automatic. It becomes a repeated habit and behavior. And so this little simple routine, trigger, routine, reward, is something that all habits share and that we should keep in mind as we progress throughout the rest of the course.
